analysis.mistake: Logo only fills first row of header instead of both rows. Planning Agent interpreted items-center from Figma as centering the logo vertically within a single row, when Figma's actual structure had nav as a sibling enabling the logo to span both rows.
analysis.expected: Logo should stretch across both the eyebrow/utility row AND the main navigation row, filling the full height.
analysis.rootCause: Planning Agent focused on individual CSS property names (items-center = centered) rather than understanding the overall Figma component hierarchy. The nav element was a sibling of the logo at the flex-row level, preventing the logo from spanning two rows. When logo and nav are siblings at the same level with equal height, the logo's self-stretch can only extend to the height of the shortest sibling (the utility row), not across a column containing two rows.

suggestions.content: When analyzing flex layouts in Figma, prioritize component hierarchy (parent/child nesting) over individual property names. Verify that elements intended to span multiple related items are siblings of a container holding those items, not siblings of the items themselves. For example, a logo spanning two rows should be a flex sibling of a column containing both rows, not a flex sibling of individual rows.
suggestions.rationale: Planning Agent misinterpreted items-center as a layout constraint instead of analyzing whether the flex structure allowed multi-row spanning, preventing correct hierarchy early.

suggestions.content: When rebuilding header/navigation structures: verify that layout elements allowing flexible height (logo, nav bars) are siblings of height-containing elements (columns with multiple rows), not siblings of individual rows. Use `items-stretch` on flex parents to ensure all flex children match the parent's full height, especially when combining elements of different natural heights.
suggestions.rationale: Header logo-spanning-rows issue revealed that flex siblings have height constraints based on the shortest sibling. Planning Agent must verify component nesting supports the intended visual spanning.

suggestions.content: When validating layout relationships in QA, verify not just individual element properties but also structural span patterns: does element X visually span elements Y and Z? Compare the component's nesting hierarchy against the Figma structure to ensure container relationships (which elements are siblings vs. children) match the design.
suggestions.rationale: Design QA validated revision 1 despite logo not spanning both rows because it checked individual properties (alignment, color) but not structural layout relationships.
